subject: social_sciences
chapter_title: Chapter 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

competencies.justification: Central — the chapter's entire architecture is organised around documenting early Indian civilisation as a case of settled urban life, covering the emergence of agriculture, food habits, construction, metallurgy, water technology, craft, trade, cultural life, and the eventual return to rural life. Every named section contributes to this overarching study of early civilisation. Removing this competency would structurally dissolve the chapter's fundamental organising purpose.

Substantive — multiple architecturally distinct sections develop the skill of interpreting multiple source types. 'The Sarasvatī River' connects Vedic textual sources with archaeological map evidence. 'The Lives of the Ancients' requires interpretation of primary artefacts (figurines, seals, pots) as historical sources. 'A Brisk Trade' uses seals and dockyard evidence as material source interpretation. The chapter's multi-section architecture makes the continuity of this competency evident across named sections.

Substantive — two dedicated named sections each constitute an architecturally distinct block explaining a major historical change and its societal impact. 'From Village to City' explains the transformation from scattered villages to planned cities (~3500–2600 BCE) as a fundamental societal change. 'The End or a New Beginning?' explains the decline around 1900 BCE and its societal impact: dispersal to rural settlements and loss of administration. Together they frame the chapter's temporal arc of rise and fall.

Substantive — 'A Brisk Trade' is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section devoted entirely to Harappan trade as an economic activity. It covers commodities (carnelian beads, shell bangles, timber, gold, cotton), production techniques (bead drilling, shell-working), trade routes (overland, river, maritime), infrastructure (dockyard at Lothal, 217 × 36 metres), and identification tools (steatite seals). The section stands alone as a complete learning unit on trade and commerce.

Present — 'The Sarasvatī River' is a named subsection whose primary subject is a natural geographical phenomenon (a river that dried up) and its spatial significance for settlement distribution. 'The End or a New Beginning?' has a dedicated passage explaining climatic change from 2200 BCE as a causal factor in the civilisation's decline. Students must perform a cognitive operation connecting natural phenomena (river systems, climate) to human settlement patterns, but these passages are structurally subordinate to the historical narrative.

Present — Named sections contain passages whose primary subject includes the distribution of resources across geographies. 'A Brisk Trade' discusses carnelian sourced from Gujarat, copper imports, and coastal trade routes indicating resource distribution. 'What Did the Harappans Eat?' links river-bank fertile soil to agricultural production. 'Water Management' describes differential water infrastructure across cities (wells in Mohenjo-daro, reservoirs in Dholavira). However, this competency is always in service of the historical narrative rather than a standalone structural purpose.

Present — 'The Lives of the Ancients' is a named section whose primary subject is Harappan material culture, and students are explicitly asked to identify cultural elements that remain familiar today — bangles covering an entire arm (still visible in Gujarat and Rajasthan), the namaste gesture, the swastika symbol, and the Dancing Girl figurine. 'The End or a New Beginning?' explicitly states that Harappan culture and technology survived and was passed on to the next phase of Indian civilisation, establishing cultural continuity.

Present — The synthesis passage 'Before we move on…' is a named structural element whose primary subject is India's civilisational contributions: the Harappan civilisation as one of the oldest in the world, creators of planned cities, efficient water management, diverse crafts, brisk trade, first cotton cultivation in Eurasia, and the first intensive maritime activity in India. Students perform a cognitive operation of recognising and summarising Indian contributions to human civilisation.
